Curriculum Digests Newsletter

Chapter 4 Consideration

Shaping the AI-Mediated Shortlist

⏱️ 60 minutes

🎯 Learning Objectives

  • Understand how AI compresses your positioning into digestible bullets
  • Craft claims that survive summarization intact
  • Frame competitive differentiation for machine comparison
  • Test your positioning against AI summarization

The Compression Problem

When a buyer asks their AI assistant "Compare the top three enterprise data platforms," your carefully crafted three-paragraph positioning gets crushed into a single bullet point. Maybe two if you're lucky.

This is the compression problem. AI agents don't reproduce your messaging — they distill it. And in that distillation, nuance evaporates. Your differentiation disappears. Your value prop becomes generic.

Key Insight

You're not writing for readers anymore. You're writing for summarizers. The test isn't "does this sound good?" It's "does this survive compression?"

What Gets Lost in Compression

  • Qualifiers and nuance — "In complex enterprise environments where data governance is critical" becomes "enterprise data platform"
  • Emotional language — "Revolutionary, game-changing, transformative" gets stripped as filler
  • Context-dependent claims — Statements that require background knowledge get simplified or dropped
  • Comparative advantages — "Better than X" requires the AI to validate X, so it often omits the comparison

Before Compression

  • "Our platform offers best-in-class data integration capabilities that help enterprises unify their data landscape"
  • "We're the leader in helping organizations achieve their digital transformation goals"

After AI Summary

  • "Data integration platform"
  • "Enterprise software vendor"

Positioning for Machines

If compression is inevitable, your job is to make the compressed version as powerful as possible. This requires a fundamental shift in how you write positioning.

The Crisp Claim Framework

Every positioning statement should pass the Crisp Claim test:

🎯 Concrete

Specific numbers, names, and facts. "150+ data sources" not "many integrations."

⚡ Remarkable

Something worth noting. If a competitor could say the same thing, it's not remarkable.

📦 Independent

Makes sense without context. Works as a standalone statement.

💬 Speakable

Natural enough to quote verbatim. How would a buyer describe you to their colleague?

✅ Provable

Can be verified against other sources. Claims that can't be checked get lower weight.

Before & After

Weak: "We provide innovative solutions for modern enterprises seeking to leverage their data assets."

Strong: "SAP Datasphere: The only platform connecting 150+ enterprise data sources with built-in AI governance."

Quotable Differentiation

When AI compares vendors, it looks for clear differentiators it can articulate. Vague advantages get flattened. Unique capabilities get preserved.

  • Categorical uniqueness — "The only platform that..." is more defensible than "the best platform for..."
  • Quantified advantages — "3x faster deployment" survives; "faster deployment" disappears
  • Architecture claims — "Built on X technology" gives AI something concrete to reference
  • Customer proof — "Used by 80% of Fortune 100" is verifiable and quotable

Competitive Framing

AI agents are comparison engines. When a buyer asks "Should I choose Vendor A or Vendor B?", the agent synthesizes available information to construct a comparison table.

Your job is to influence what appears in your column of that table — and what criteria the table even includes.

Setting the Comparison Criteria

The most powerful positioning doesn't just win on existing criteria — it introduces new criteria where you excel.

📊 Reframe the Category

Don't compete on their terms. "Unlike traditional ETL tools, Datasphere provides..." shifts the comparison frame.

🏷️ Name Your Advantage

Create terminology for your unique capability. If you name it, AI can reference it.

📈 Quantify Everything

Numbers create comparison points. "60% faster" gives AI a concrete dimension to include.

🎪 Emphasize Gaps

What can you do that competitors literally cannot? Make these binary differences explicit.

The vendor who defines the comparison criteria usually wins the comparison.

Competitive Content Strategy

AI agents pull from comparison pages, analyst reports, and head-to-head content. You should have:

  • Comparison pages — "[Your Product] vs [Competitor]" pages that AI can directly reference
  • Alternative pages — "Best [Competitor] alternatives" content you control
  • Category definitions — Content that establishes what criteria matter in your category
  • Migration guides — "Switching from X to [Your Product]" signals you're the upgrade

Testing Your Position

Before publishing, run your positioning through AI summarization. What survives? What disappears? What gets distorted?

The Summarization Stress Test

  1. Feed your positioning to multiple AI tools — "Summarize this company's value proposition in 2 sentences"
  2. Request comparison — "Compare [Your Product] to [Competitor] based on this information"
  3. Ask for bullets — "List the 3 main differentiators of this product"
  4. Test recall — Give the AI other information, then ask "What was unique about [Your Product]?"

Red Flags

If the AI summary says "enterprise platform," "innovative solution," or "helps organizations achieve their goals" — your positioning is too generic. The summary should include your specific differentiators.

Iterative Refinement

Test → Analyze what survived → Strengthen weak points → Test again. This is now a standard content QA process.

  • If a key differentiator gets dropped, make it more concrete
  • If a claim gets distorted, simplify the language
  • If you're indistinguishable from competitors, add quantified uniqueness
  • If emotional language disappears, that's fine — replace with facts

🎯 Interactive Exercise: Summarization Stress Test

Put your positioning through the compression gauntlet:

  1. Gather your current positioning — Product description, value prop, key differentiators
  2. Test with ChatGPT:
    • "Summarize this product in one sentence"
    • "What makes this different from competitors?"
    • "List 3 key capabilities"
  3. Score the results:
    • Did your main differentiator survive? (Critical)
    • Are the claims specific or generic? (Important)
    • Could this describe a competitor? (Fail if yes)
  4. Rewrite weak elements using the Crisp Claim framework
  5. Test again — iterate until your differentiation survives

Time: ~25 minutes

Key Takeaways

  • AI compresses your positioning — write for summarizers, not readers
  • Crisp claims (Concrete, Remarkable, Independent, Speakable, Provable) survive compression
  • Competitive framing is about setting criteria, not just winning on existing ones
  • Comparison and alternative pages give AI content to reference
  • Summarization stress testing should be standard content QA
